coredata.dc:title: A novel and facile synthesis of carbon quantum dots via salep hydrothermal treatment as the silver nanoparticles support: Application to electroanalytical determination of H2O2 in fetal bovine serum
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Jahanbakhshi, Mojtaba
Habibi, Biuck
coredata.dc:description: A simple, low-cost, and green process was used for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) through the hydrothermal treatment of salep as a novel bio-polymeric carbon source in presence of only pure water. The silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) were embedded on the surface of CQDs by ultra-violate (UV) irradiation to the CQDs and silver nitrate mixture solution. The as-synthesized CQDs and AgNPs decorated CQDs nanohybrid (AgNPs/CQDs) were characterized by UV–vis and photoluminescence spectroscopy, Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, atomic force microcopy, X-ray diffraction, and field emission scanning electron microscopy. Then, the AgNPs/CQDs nanohybrid was casted on the glassy carbon electrode in order to prepare an amperometric hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) sensor. The electrochemical investigations show that the AgNPs/CQDs nanohybrid possesses an excellent performance toward the H2O2 reduction. In the optimum condition, the linear range of H2O2 determination was achieved from 0.2 to 27.0μM with high sensitivity (1.5μA/µM) and the limit of detection was obtained about 80nM (S/N=3). Finally, the prepared nanohybrid modified electrode was effectively applied to the H2O2 detection in the disinfected fetal bovine serum samples, and the recovery was obtained about 98%. The achieved results indicate that the AgNPs/CQDs nanohybrid with high reproducibility, repeatability, and stability has a favorable capability in electrochemical sensors improvement.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Hydrothermal method
Salep
Silver nanoparticles
Electrochemical sensor
Hydrogen peroxide

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref
xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQDs and AgNPs/CQDs nanohybrid
Direct electrocatalysis and amperometric determination of H2O2
Anti-interference studies and real sample analysis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: the research office of Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to the research office of Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University (Grant No. 214/d/10802 ) for its financial support. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs sample solution (A), photographs of the CQDs solution in daylight (B) and excited via UV lamp at 365nm (B), and emission spectra of the CQDs solution at various excitation wavelengths as specified on figure (C).
TEM image of the as-synthesized CQDs (A), AFM image of CQDs (B), and XRD pattern of CQDs (C).
TEM image of the AgNPs/CQDs nanohybrid (A) and the XRD pattern of AgNPs/CQDs (B).
Cyclic voltammograms of AgNPs/CQDs/GCE (curve a), CQDs/GCE (curve b), and bare GCE (curve c) in 0.1M PBS pH 7.0 and in presence of 0.1mM H2O2. Scan rate is 100mVs−1.

(A) Amperometric responses of AgNPs/CQDs/GCE to successive additions of H2O2 (0.2–1.0µM) in 0.1M PBS pH 7.0 and the plot of response current vs. H2O2 concentration (inset; the standard deviations obtained by five repeated measurements were shown as the error bars.). (B) Amperometric response of AgNPs/CQDs/GCE to addition of 2µM of H2O2 (first and last additions) and 0.1mM AA, Dp, Ep, UA, CA, and L-Tyr. Amperometric conditions; −0.20V constant applied potential and 1000rpm rotation speed.

head.ce:title: A novel and facile synthesis of carbon quantum dots via salep hydrothermal treatment as the silver nanoparticles support: Application to electroanalytical determination of H2O2 in fetal bovine serum

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electroanalytical Chemistry Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, Tabriz 53714-161, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Electroanalytical Chemistry Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple, low-cost, and green process was used for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) through the hydrothermal treatment of salep as a novel bio-polymeric carbon source in presence of only pure water. The silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) were embedded on the surface of CQDs by ultra-violate (UV) irradiation to the CQDs and silver nitrate mixture solution. The as-synthesized CQDs and AgNPs decorated CQDs nanohybrid (AgNPs/CQDs) were characterized by UV–vis and photoluminescence spectroscopy, Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, atomic force microcopy, X-ray diffraction, and field emission scanning electron microscopy. Then, the AgNPs/CQDs nanohybrid was casted on the glassy carbon electrode in order to prepare an amperometric hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) sensor. The electrochemical investigations show that the AgNPs/CQDs nanohybrid possesses an excellent performance toward the H2O2 reduction. In the optimum condition, the linear range of H2O2 determination was achieved from 0.2 to 27.0μM with high sensitivity (1.5μA/µM) and the limit of detection was obtained about 80nM (S/N=3). Finally, the prepared nanohybrid modified electrode was effectively applied to the H2O2 detection in the disinfected fetal bovine serum samples, and the recovery was obtained about 98%. The achieved results indicate that the AgNPs/CQDs nanohybrid with high reproducibility, repeatability, and stability has a favorable capability in electrochemical sensors improvement.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a novel kind of carbon nanostructures with dimensions below 10nm. For the first time, CQDs were discovered accidently during the synthesis, separation, and purification processes of single-walled carbon nanotubes, using the electrophoresis method (Xu et al., 2004). Thereafter, several synthesis processes such as chemical ablation, laser ablation, electrochemical carbonization, combustion, microwave processing, silica template methods, deoxygenation of natural carbon sources, microwave irradiation, and hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment have been reported to prepare CQDs (Zhang et al., 2015; Han et al., 2015; Li et al., 2015a; Wang and Hu, 2014). Among them, hydrothermal treatment in presence of the appropriate precursor is recognized to be one of the facile and low-cost approaches due to its use as an economical device, simple and one-step operation, super selectivity, and high yielding (Niu et al., 2015; Xiao et al., 2015; Lim et al., 2015; Liang et al., 2013; Atienzar et al., 2013; Ouyang et al., 2013; Liu et al., 2012). In this regard, bio-polymers such as chitosan (Yang et al., 2012), gelatin (Liang et al., 2013), and alginate (Atienzar et al., 2013) have revealed a high capability as the precursors. On the other hand, salep as a polysaccharide with numerous advantages, such as inexpensiveness (Chung et al., 2012), non-toxicity (Pourjavadi et al., 2013), high availability in nature (Ktistis and Georgakopoulos, 1991), and gelling properties (Pourjavadi et al., 2012) has similar structure and properties to chitosan and gelatin (Bardajee and Hooshyar, 2014). Inspired by this, we supposed that this bio-polymer, like chitosan and gelatin, can provide a green and highly available precursor for the synthesis of CQDs via the hydrothermal process.

In the past decade, CQDs have been found to be promising nanomaterials for various applications in catalysis, electro and photocatalysis, sensors, bio-sensor, bio-medicine, bio-imaging, optoelectronics, drug delivery, photodynamic therapy, and surface-enhanced Raman scattering due to their benign abundant inexpensive nature, optical properties, their fluorescence emissions in particular, and other exclusive properties (Qian et al., 2015; Lim et al., 2015; Shereema et al., 2015; Yang et al., 2015a, 2015b; Han et al., 2013a, 2013b; Li et al., 2012; Baker and Baker, 2010; Liu et al., 2015). So that, there have been increasing attentions to the design, fabrication, and use of the CQDs-based hybrids, composites, and doped nanomaterials as the excellent matters for sensors, bio-sensors, and electrocatalysts preparation (Wang and Hu, 2014; Hong et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2015a, 2015b; Tang et al., 2014; Yu et al., 2002; Ouyang et al., 2013; Lu et al., 2011). It is found that CQDs have several advantages as the components of the nanoscale devices in the sensing areas, such as low toxicity, water solubility, superior physicochemical stability, excellent bio-compatibility, good cell permeability, high photostability, higher sensitivity as labels in the lateral flow assays, excitation-dependent multicolor emission, promising performance as the fluorescent probes in the immunoassays detection of small bio-analytes like anti-bacterial drugs, environmentally friendly, and so on; render them very attractive for sensing applications. Flexibility in surface modification and also abundant functional groups (–OH, –COOH, etc.) on the surface of CQDs provide many favorable sites for the fabrication of various CQDs-based nanomaterials. Besides, some studies disclose that the photo-excited CQDs, as a new class of the carbon nanomaterials, are admirable electron donors’ species (Wang et al., 2009; Han et al., 2013a, 2013b; Cao et al., 2011). High electron-donating capacity of the photo-excited CQDs facilitates the reduction of metal ions to related metal nanoparticles on the surface of CQDs to fabricate different metallic and metallic oxide nanoparticles hybrids and composites (Choi et al., 2013). As well, because of the low-temperature processability of the synthesis procedures and good hydrophilicity, the CQDs are compatible with large-area, roll-to-roll mass production techniques and are suitable for printed electronic devices (Choi et al., 2013). Therefore, the mentioned photo-induced electron transfer properties accompanied by high stability, abundant surface functional groups, and high surface area of CQDs as the heterogeneous supports introduce these nanostructures as the proper electrocatalysts, sensors, and bio-sensors materials (Huang et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2015; Yang et al., 2015a, 2015b; Li et al., 2015b; Zhu et al., 2013; Martindale et al., 2015). On the other hand, silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) with profuse practical capacities have attracted significant attentions because of their noble metal nanoparticles characteristics and excellent electrocatalytic activity, especially toward H2O2 reduction (Chen et al., 2013a, 2013b; Tian et al., 2010; Sophi and Muralidharan, 2014; Habibi and Jahanbakhshi, 2015). Over the past few years, various AgNPs-based composites or hybrids such as AgNPs decorated on graphene oxide nano-sheets (Sreekantha et al., 2016), on nano-diamonds (Habibi and Jahanbakhshi, 2015), on nano-polypyrrole (Moozarm Nia et al., 2015), on carbon nanotubes (Lin et al., 2014), and on reduced graphene oxide (Yang et al., 2016) have been designed and extensively investigated as the electrocatalysts for the H2O2 determination. Therefore, to take the benefits of both CQDs and AgNPs and to integrate unique properties of each material and interactional features between them (Li et al., 2011; Choi et al., 2013; Yang et al., 2015a, 2015b) to bring out novel properties in the sensor applications, in the present work, we decided to fabricate a nanocomposite based on AgNPs and CQDs (AgNPs/CQDs) in which the CQDs, as the support, supply extensive surface area over which AgNPs can be extremely dispersed and stabilized.

Herein, for the first time, CQDs were synthesized from the bio-polymer of salep using the hydrothermal process. Then, AgNPs/CQDs were prepared by ultra-violate (UV) irradiation to the CQDs and silver nitrate mixture solution. High dispersibility of the obtained AgNPs/CQDs nanohybrid in the aqueous solution leads to formation of the stable suspension, which is capable of creating a uniform stable thin film on the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface. The prepared nanohybrid modified electrode, AgNPs/CQDs/GCE, was applied for the electrochemical determination of H2O2. Cyclic voltammetry and chronoamperometry were used for the electrochemical studies and determination of H2O2, respectively. Fundamentally, by combining the capabilities of AgNPs and CQDs, the planned sensor reveals admirable performance for determination of H2O2 with high stability, high sensitivity, wide linear dynamic range, low detection limit, and satisfactory reproducibility and repeatability.

Salep was purchased from the local store and CQDs were synthesized from salep through the hydrothermal process. AgNO3, H2O2 (30%, v/v aqueous solution), and other chemicals were obtained from Merck or Fluka and applied without any additional purification. Chitosan with low molecular weight (Mw 4000) was obtained from Aldrich and used as received. Disinfected fetal bovine serum was obtained from Razi Institute of Vaccine and Serum (Tehran-Iran). The 0.1M phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.0) was used as the supporting electrolyte and prepared from stock solutions of 0.1M H3PO4, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, and NaOH. Doubly distilled water was used in all of the experiments.

The electrochemical studies were performed via an AUTOLAB PGSTAT-100 (potentiostat/galvanostat) (http://www.metrohm-autolab.com) at room temperature. The operated three-electrode system was organized of a silver-silver chloride electrode [Ag/AgCl/KCl (3.0M)], a platinum wire, and AgNPs/CQDs/GCE respectively as the reference, auxiliary, and working electrodes. The absorbent and fluorescent investigations were carried out using a T80 UV–vis spectrophotometer (PG Instrument Ltd, England) (http://www.pginstruments.com) and FP-6200 spectrofluorometer (JASCO Corporation, Tokyo, Japan) (http://www.jasco.co.jp), respectively. The morphology of CQDs and AgNPs/CQDs was studied using the JSM-6700F field emission-scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) (JEOL Ltd., Japan) (http://www.jeolusa.com), FLEX AFM C3000 atomic force microscopy (AFM) (http://www.nanosurf.com), and JEM-1200 EX/S transmission electron microscopy (TEM) apparatuses (http://www.jeolusa.com). The XRD investigations were done with a Bruker AXS (D8 Advance) (http://www.bruker.com) X-ray diffractometer. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) studies were carried out by a Bruker model Vector 22 FT-IR Spectrometer (http://www.bruker.com).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs and AgNPs/CQDs nanohybrid
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were prepared through a simple hydrothermal carbonization of salep as the carbon source. At first, 2.0g of salep was solved in 50mL of pure water. Then, the obtained solution was transferred to a 50mL stainless steel autoclave and located in a furnace at 180°C for about 12h, during which the solution changed from a colorless liquid to a brownish-yellow solution, indicating the formation of CQDs. After that, the autoclave was cooled at room temperature. In order to remove any solid particles such as weight and large precipitate and agglomerated particles, the resultant solution was centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 30min. The supernatant brownish-yellow solution of CQDs was separated and kept at 4°C for further characterization and use.

The AgNPs/CQDs nanohybrid was prepared according to the report by Choi and co-workers (Choi et al., 2013). A solution of CQDs (2mg/ml) and silver nitrate (5mg/ml) mixture was made and located in a quartz cuvette (1×1×5cm). The cuvette was exposed to the UV light (λ=254nm) emitted from a mercury lamp for 20min. After the UV irradiation, the solution color altered from brownish-yellow to dark brown, inferring the reduction of Ag ions to Ag nanoparticles on CQDs. Excellent electron-donating capability of photo-excited CQDs enables fast reduction of Ag+ to the corresponding metal nanoparticles (AgNPs) on the surface of CQDs, which resulted in the emergence and rapid increase of the surface plasmon absorption, owing to the high amount of AgNPs produced by photo-reduction (Wang et al., 2009). No AgNP formation was found in the repeated control experiment in the absence of CQDs, as expected.

Before modification, GCE was polished to be mirror-like with 1.0μm alumina slurry on a polishing cloth, rinsed successively with doubly distilled water, sonicated in doubly distilled water for 10min, and then dried at room temperature. For electrode modification, 5µL of the obtained AgNPs/CQDs nanohybrid was placed on the cleaned GCE surface and dried in air. Finally, 2µL of CS (0.2% in 3% acetic acid aqueous solution) was dropped on the above-mentioned modified GCE surface and dried at room temperature for about 5h. CS was used as the fixative to form a strong film to modify the electrode. The obtained modified electrode was washed carefully with doubly distilled water before use.

In order to study the CQDs optical behavior, UV–vis absorption and photoluminescent (PL) methods were applied in details. The CQDs solution shows a strong UV–vis absorption peak placed at 280nm, which is a usual characteristic of CQDs and arises from the π–π* transition of nanocarbon particles ( Fig. 1A) (Liang et al., 2013; Hsu and Chang, 2012). The photograph and color of the CQD solution is brownish-yellow and transparent under daylight and reveals strong green luminescence under UV irradiation (Fig. 1B and C, respectively). Fig. 1D shows the PL spectrums of solution at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 320 to 500nm. When excite at 320nm, the CQDs show the maximum emission at 470nm with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of about 80nm. As reported in the literature (Shi et al., 2014; Dong et al., 2012), from the PL spectra, the FWHM parameter can be used as the convenient index of size distribution (smaller FWHM is indicative of narrower distribution). The obtained FWHM in this study implies narrower particles size distribution of the as-synthesized CQDs (Chen et al., 2013a, 2013b). In addition, the excitation-dependent PL behavior of CQDs, which is similar to the previously reported CQDs (Yang et al., 2012), can be clearly perceived (Fig. 1D). When the excitation wavelengths change from 320 to 500nm, the emission peaks shift to longer wavelengths (bathochromic shift of emission from CQDs) and the PL intensity decreases gradually. The excitation-dependent emission is a typical characteristic of CQDs (Chen et al., 2013a, 2013b; Liang et al., 2013; Shi et al., 2014) ( Fig. 1).

Fig. 2A illustrates the TEM image of CQDs. It can be obviously perceived that the synthesized CQDs have closely spherical shape with a narrow size distribution of about 5–8nm in diameter. The structure of CQDs was further characterized by AFM. The AFM image of CQDs (Fig. 2B) suggests that the height of these particles were about 4–8nm, which is in agreement with the TEM characterization. Also, Fig. 2C displays the XRD pattern of the prepared CQDs. The broad diffraction peak placed around 2θ=23° reveals an amorphous nature for the resulted CQDs (Liang et al., 2013) (Fig. 2).

The FT-IR analysis also was used to structural and componential characterization of the as-synthesized CQDs. Fig. 3 shows the FT-IR spectrum of CQDs. The typical absorption band of O–H at 3420cm−1, the C–O stretching vibration band at 1109cm−1, the C=O stretching vibration band at 1707cm−1, and the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of C–O–C (≈1300cm−1 and ≈1200cm−1) signify the presence of carboxylic acid and other oxygen functional groups on the as-synthesized CQDs (Yang et al., 2015a, 2015b, 2012; Li et al., 2014; Liang et al., 2013). Furthermore, three distinctive peaks at 2962, 1458, and 1364cm−1 are related to the C–H, C=C, and C–C stretching vibrations, respectively, indicating the presence of alkyl and aryl groups (Liu et al., 2012). Having these functional groups beside the high electron-donating feature introduces CQDs as a favorable electrocatalytic support (Fig. 3)

The CQDs and AgNPs/CQDs modified GCE surfaces were characterized by the FE-SEM method. The FE-SEM image of CQDs (see Fig. S1A in the supplementary materials) displays the spherical shape of CQDs on the GCE surface. Also, the FE-SEM image reveals that the AgNPs’ distribution on the surface of CQDs is in the shape of a uniform film with the spherical mold (see Fig. S1B in the supplementary materials). On the other hand, a typical TEM image of the AgNPs/CQDs nanocomposite is presented in Fig. 4A. As can be seen, the AgNPs/CQDs nanocomposite particles have average diameters around 10nm. Furthermore, the XRD technique was used for characterization of AgNPs/CQDs. Fig. 4B shows the XRD result of the prepared AgNPs/CQDs. Four-peaks located at 39°, 46.1°, 66°, and 79.5° appear for the AgNPs/CQDs nanohybrid, in which the diffraction peaks have respectable accordance with Ag particles (111), (200), (220) and (311) planes, respectively. All XRD patterns were indexed based on the JCPDS file (Swarthmore, 1991). Despite the long usage history of nanostructured carbon supported metal nanoparticles, the knowledges about the metal-carbon interactions have been complicated by inhomogeneity and complexity of the carbon nanomaterials (Yan, et al., 2012). It is widely believed that: i) anchoring of the metal nanoparticles on nanocarbon supports is due to the presence of oxygen-containing functions, such as carboxylic, carbonyl, and phenolic groups on them and ii) the affinity between the metal nanoparticles and pristine nanocarbons even in the absence of oxygen containing groups are two fundamental factors in their interactions (Toebes et al., 2001; Chan et al., 2008). Therefore, the interaction between the p/π orbitals of C and d orbitals of Ag in AgNPs, and the interactions between the functional groups on CQDs (–OH, C=O) and AgNPs are responsible for the formation and high stability of the prepared AgNPs/CQDs composite (Liu et al., 2014) (Fig. 4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Direct electrocatalysis and amperometric determination of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: The cyclic voltammetry and amperometry techniques were used to investigate the AgNPs/CQDs/GCE performance toward H2O2 electro-reduction and its electroanalytical determination, respectively. The cyclic voltammograms of AgNPs/CQDs/GCE (curve a), CQDs/GCE (curve b), and bare GCE (curve c) in the 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0) containing 0.1mM H2O2 are displayed in Fig. 5. Curve a shows the AgNPs/CQDs/GCE electrocatalytic response to the H2O2 reduction. It can be realized that AgNPs/CQDs/GCE shows a significant reduction peak current, nearly about 25µA, at above −0.35V vs. [Ag/AgCl/KCl (3.0M)], whereas no distinct current response of H2O2 is observed at the bare GCE (curve c) in the studied potential range of −0.10 to −0.8V, while at CQDs/GCE, there is a certain increase in the reduction current (curve b). In this condition, the observed current at CQDs/GCE is produced by the reduction of H2O2, since the CQDs have negligible current response in this potential range in the electrolyte solution without H2O2. Compared with the bare GCE response, this increase in current response indicates that the electrochemical reduction of H2O2 could be slightly enhanced by CQDs due to their presence and minor electrocatalytic activity (Yang et al., 2015a, 2015b). As the reduction current of H2O2 at AgNPs/CQDs/GCE is remarkably larger than that of CQDs/GCE, the difference is surely engendered by the electrocatalytic reduction effect of AgNPs toward H2O2, signifying the sensitivity of AgNPs/CQDs/GCE toward H2O2 electro-reduction. These results prove that the AgNPs decorated on CQDs act as promising electrocatalysts toward H2O2 reduction and their notable catalytic response can be attributed to the massive AgNPs amount dispersed on the prepared nanocomposite. In other words, the significant and admirable catalytic performance of AgNPs/CQDs/GCE toward H2O2 reduction may arise from excellent catalytic activity of AgNPs toward H2O2 reduction, novel integrated properties which come from compositing CQDs and AgNPs, and also from the principal role of CQDs as an ideal support that creates a high surface area over which AgNPs can be extremely dispersed and stabilized (Fig. 5).

The obtained results make it clear that the peak current of H2O2 electro-reduction depends on the amount of the AgNPs/CQDs nanohybrid suspension casted on the GCE surface (see Fig. S2 in the supplementary materials). By increasing the amount of AgNPs/CQDs, the response current of the sensor grows consequently, which may arise from enlargement of the modified electrode surface area. The sensor response reaches the maximum value of nearly 5μL and then shows insignificant change up to 7μL, and when the suspension volume exceeds 7μL, the peak current drops. In fact, when the film is thin, the amount of analyte diffused on the electrode surface is low, thus the peak current is small. On the other hand, when the modifier film thinness is excessively thick, the film conductivity decreases. Furthermore, when the amount of the casted nanohybrid increases, the current of background similarly enhances; therefore, signal to the noise (S/N) ratio varies. In view of the response current and the S/N ratio, 5μL of the AgNPs/CQDs suspension was used to modify the GCE surface.

More investigations were done to identify the H2O2 transport features to the AgNPs/CQDs/GCE surface. For this purpose, the cyclic voltammograms were recorded using AgNPs/CQDs/GCE in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0) and in presence of 0.1mM H2O2 at different scan rates. The corresponding cathodic peak currents were directly proportional to the scan rate in the range of 10–200mV/s (not shown here). The plot of the H2O2 reduction response vs. the scan rate square root displays a straight line, inferring that the H2O2 reduction is controlled by analyte diffusion, which is an ideal characteristic for the quantitative determinations (Han et al., 2013a, 2013b).

According to the literature (Li et al., 2013a, 2013b; Zhao et al. 2009 ; Welch et al., 2005; He et al., 2012), the proposed mechanism of the H2O2 reduction on the AgNPs-based modified electrode surface can be shown as the following equations:
H

2

A g N P s
1

H
2
O

Firstly, H2O2 was decomposed into H2O and O2 under the AgNPs catalyzer and then the generated O2 would turn into the sensor detection signal. On the other hand, the oxygen electro-reduction at the modified electrode surface can occur via the following mechanism:
O

Aforementioned cyclic voltammetric data show that AgNPs/CQDs/GCE facilitates the low potential amperometric detection of H2O2. The amperometric study examines this possibility by comparing the current–time tracings at −0.2V using the present modified electrode to successive additions of H2O2 with different concentrations in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0). As expected from the cyclic voltammetric profiles, the AgNPs/CQDs/GCE sensor responds sensitively to each addition, yielding steady-state signals. The typical current–time plot of AgNPs/CQDs/GCE at the constant potential (−0.2V) on successive H2O2 concentration additions is exposed in Fig. 6A. AgNPs/CQDs/GCE givess a sensitive and rapid response to the H2O2 concentration addition. It is realized that the steady-state signals reach stable value in less than 2s, showing a fast amperometric response performance. The obtained linear range is about 0.2µM to 27.0µM with the linear equation of: I (µM)=−1.6143 CHydrogen peroxide (µM) −10.666 (R 2= 0.9937) (inset of Fig. 6). The sensitivity and detection limit of the prepared sensor toward H2O2 are estimated to be 1.5µA/µM and 80nM(S/N=3), respectively.

Until now, different kinds of carbon nanostructures such as multi-walled carbon nanotubes (CNTs) (Habibi et al., 2012), mesoporous carbon (MC) (Habibi and Jahanbakhshi, 2014), and diamond nanoparticles (DNPs) (Habibi and Jahanbakhshi, 2015) as the AgNPs support for fabricating H2O2 sensors were reported by our research group. Referring to their electroanalytical performances, it is clear that CQDs, as the AgNPs support, are comparable to the other ones (Sreekantha et al., 2016; Moozarm Nia et al., 2015; Lin et al., 2014; Yang et al., 2016). But, compared to the above-mentioned carbon nanoparticles, the CQDs preference is their simpler and greener synthesis rout that needs no complicated technique or particular chemical. Besides, compared to the aforementioned carbon nanostructures, decoration of CQDs with AgNPs performs through a faster and simpler process, only via 20min UV irradiation without any special reagent requirement.

The reproducibility, repeatability, and stability of a sensor are very important for its use in real sample detection. The reproducibility, repeatability, and long-term stability of the present sensor were evaluated by measuring its response in the optimum conditions (PBS, pH 7.0, E applied=−0.2V). Firstly, the AgNPs/CQDs/GCE reproducibility was studied in 5µM H2O2 solution for five determinations. The estimated relative standard deviation (RSD) was about 2.9%. In the same way, to estimate the modified electrode repeatability, three independent AgNPs/CQDs/GCE sensors were used to measure the concentration of 5µM H2O2. The RSD was obtained about 2.7%, which discloses the excellent sensor fabrication process repeatability. Moreover, the long-term stability of AgNPs/CQDs/GCE was examined as follows: after that the response of a typical modified electrode was recorded in 5µM H2O2 solution, the used modified electrode was rinsed with distilled water and kept in PBS (pH 7.0). After 10 and 30 days, the decreased response values were obtained about 1.9% and 6.0%, respectively. Therefore, the present sensor has good long-term stability, repeatability, and reproducibility; it could be used for repetitive detections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-interference studies and real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Several chemical compounds potentially existing in biological liquids or other real samples were investigated for their interference on the amperometric determination of 2µM H2O2. Ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine(DP), epinephrine (EP), uric acid (UA), salicylic acid (SA), and L-tyrosine (L-Tyr) interfering effects on the amperometric determination of H2O2 was examined and displayed in Fig. 6B. The resulted sensor shows no amperometric signal toward 0.1mM of the mentioned compounds. But a well-defined H2O2 response was obtained after the addition of 2µM H2O2 in the first and last steps, respectively. The selectivity of the AgNPs/CQDs/GCE sensor and its resistance to interference were due to the low working potential (−0.2V) in amperometric determination of H2O2 (Fig. 6).

Finally, the designed sensor was used for determination of H2O2 in disinfected fetal bovine serum (FBS). FBS was prepared through a sequence of membrane filtering and centrifuging. The filtration step includes the use of three 0.1μm sterilizing grade membrane filters in series. Then, 5% (v/v) methanol solution was added to the filtrated FBS samples and centrifuged in 8000rpm for about 30min. All of the obtained samples were diluted with PBS (pH 7.0) and spiked with H2O2 standard solutions in the range of 1.0–15.0µM. The amperometric technique was employed for sample analysis. The results have been presented in Table S1 (see Table S1in the supplementary materials).The average recovery was estimated to be about 98%.

In this work, a novel nanohybrid has been prepared to fabricate a non-enzymatic H2O2 sensor based on AgNPs embedded on CQDs. CQDs were synthesized through the hydrothermal carbonization of salep as a novel carbon source in presence of only pure water for the first time. Then, AgNPs were simply decorated on the CQDs surface by UV irradiation to CQDs and AgNO3 mixture solution. CQDs show high performance as a suitable catalyst support and cause the creation of small size of AgNPs. The obtained results revealed that the AgNPs/CQDs nanohybrid has admirable catalytic activity toward H2O2 electro-reduction and can be applied to prepare a non-enzymatic H2O2 sensor with excellent electrochemical features such as high stability and sensitivity, respectable selectivity, and desirable reproducibility.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the research office of Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University (Grant No. 214/d/10802) for its financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2016.02.064.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Swarthmore, P.A., 1991, JCPDS International Center for Diffraction Data.
